items.passage: Bioluminescence is a natural phenomenon in which living organisms create their own light through internal chemical reactions. It occurs when a substance called luciferin reacts with oxygen inside living cells. Many ocean creatures use this glow to attract mates, confuse predators, or lure prey in darkness. Deep-sea anglerfish, for example, dangle a glowing lure above their mouths to draw curious fish closer. Fireflies use a similar strategy, flashing patterns of light to signal potential partners at night.

Urban heat islands form when cities replace natural vegetation with pavement, buildings, and other heat-absorbing surfaces. These materials absorb sunlight during the day and slowly release the stored heat at night. As a result, temperatures in city centers can rise several degrees higher than in surrounding rural areas. This effect can increase energy demand for air conditioning and worsen air quality in dense neighborhoods. Planting trees, installing green roofs, and using lighter-colored materials are widely recommended to reduce this warming effect.

Habit formation is the gradual process by which repeated behaviors become automatic responses to specific situations. Each time a behavior is repeated in a similar context, the brain strengthens the neural pathway involved. Over weeks or months, this process can turn a deliberate action into an unconscious routine. Cues in the environment, such as a certain time of day, often trigger these automatic behaviors. Understanding this process can help people build healthier routines and break patterns that no longer serve them.

Long before satellites existed, sailors relied on stars and ocean currents to navigate open water. Skilled navigators used the position of the sun and stars to estimate their latitude at sea. A tool called the astrolabe measured the angle of the sun above the horizon. Later, the magnetic compass was introduced, allowing sailors to determine direction even when the sky was cloudy or overcast. These tools, combined with detailed charts, gradually made long ocean voyages safer and more predictable for traders.

Geysers are rare natural features that periodically shoot boiling water and steam high into the air. Underground, water seeps into cracks and comes into contact with extremely hot volcanic rock below the surface. As pressure builds, the superheated water rises quickly and bursts upward through a narrow opening. This sudden release of energy produces a dramatic eruption of hot water and steam above the ground. Yellowstone National Park in the United States contains the largest concentration of active geysers in the world.

The solar system consists of the sun, eight planets, and countless smaller objects bound together by gravity. Each planet moves along its own elliptical path around the sun, a pattern first described by Kepler centuries ago. Some planets are rocky and dense, while others are enormous balls of gas and ice. Beyond Neptune lies a distant region filled with icy bodies, including dwarf planets and countless small comets. Scientists study these distant objects to better understand how the solar system formed.
